questions.question: What brain network activates at bedtime, causing worries to feel louder in a quiet, dark room?

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system
The default mode network (DMN)
The reticular activating system
The prefrontal cortex

questions.explanation: The default mode network (DMN) activates when you are not focused on the external world. It specializes in self-referential thinking, planning, and rumination -- which is why worries feel louder at bedtime.

questions.question: What are the three steps of the '3 C's' technique for managing racing thoughts?

options.text: Count, Control, Calm
Catch, Check, Change the Channel
Close, Center, Clear
Contain, Challenge, Choose

questions.explanation: The 3 C's are: Catch the thought (notice it without judgment), Check the thought (ask if it is helpful right now), and Change the Channel (redirect attention to something calming).

questions.question: How does Scheduled Worry Time help with nighttime anxiety?

options.text: It suppresses worries completely
It gives worries a designated appointment earlier in the day so the brain can let go at night
It trains you to never worry again
It replaces worries with positive thoughts

questions.explanation: Scheduled Worry Time works by giving your worries a designated appointment earlier in the day. At bedtime, you can remind yourself the worry will be handled at the next scheduled time, giving your brain permission to let go.

questions.question: How does Progressive Muscle Relaxation (PMR) help quiet racing thoughts?

options.text: It tires out the muscles so the body falls asleep faster
It activates the parasympathetic nervous system and gives the brain a non-threatening focus
It raises heart rate to burn off nervous energy
It blocks the default mode network entirely

questions.explanation: PMR works on two levels: physically, it activates the parasympathetic nervous system (rest and digest mode); mentally, focusing on each muscle group gives the brain something specific and non-threatening to attend to, displacing anxious thinking.

questions.question: Why does the Cognitive Shuffle technique help with sleep?

options.text: It forces the brain to solve complex puzzles
It occupies the mind with random, unconnected imagery, signaling there is nothing important to process
It increases melatonin production
It activates the sympathetic nervous system

questions.explanation: The Cognitive Shuffle occupies the mind with random, unconnected imagery (like visualizing words starting with a letter). Because the images are meaningless, the brain signals that nothing important is happening and eventually loses interest, allowing sleep to come.
